rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, February 10, 2025, line 12, HEARING OF CLAIM OF EXEMPTION AS TO RESPONDENT MARIA FERNANDA DEL GAISO, ONLY This matter is on calendar to address the claim of exemption filed by Maria Fernanda Del Gaiso. Hearing required. Ms. Del Gaiso has not provided a financial statement form showing that the funds are necessary for her support. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. If the court adopts the tentative ruling, it will execute a judicial council form of order reflecting the ruling. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL VS. SANTOS & URRUTIA ASSOCIATES, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Moion To Compel Plaiontiffs City And County Of San Francisco And People Of H State If Ca
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, February 10, 2025, line 15, DEFENDANT KEVIN O'CONNOR'S Motion To Compel Plaintiffs' City And County Of San Francisco And People Of The State Of California'S Discovery Responses The court denies without prejudice Defendant Kevin O'Connor's motion to compel the City and County of San Francisco to provide further responses to discovery. O'Connor has not provided a separate statement in support of the motion to compel (see California Rules of Court, Rule 3.1345(a)). This is more than a technicality; separate statements help the court quickly focus on the discovery in dispute and the basis for each party's position. O'Connor has also not complied with the statutory notice period of Code of Civil Procedure 1005(b) ("Unless otherwise ordered or specifically provided by law, all moving and supporting papers shall be served and filed at least 16 court days before the hearing."). O'Connor may cure these defects in a new motion. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CVA)

The Dimalanta declaration shows counsel have not met and conferred in person, by telephone, or by video conference, as required by CCP 435.5. rulings.Case Title: RAYMOND VINCENT DIGIACOMO JR. VS. UNION SQUARE BUSINESS IMPROVEMENT DISTRICT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Defendants City And County Of San Francisco And Carla Short To Require Plaintiff To Furnish Security Pursuant To C.C.P. Section 391.1 In The Amount Of $50,000
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, February 10, 2025, line 3, DEFENDANTS CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, CARLA SHORT'S Motion Of Defendants City And County Of San Francisco And Carla Short To Require Plaintiff To Furnish Security Pursuant To C.C.P. Section 391.1 In The Amount Of $50,000 The unopposed motion of City & County of San Francisco and defendant Carla Short (collectively City) for an order requiring vexatious litigant Raymond V. DiGiacomo to furnish security pursuant to CCP 391.1 is denied without prejudice. The City contends that there is no need for it to prove that plaintiff DiGiacomo is a vexatious litigant in the instant case in light of the court's order in Case No. CGC-20-582151. The City offers CCP 391.1(b)(4) as its authority. That subdivision states that a vexatious litigant includes a person who "[h]as previously been declared to be a vexatious litigant by any state or federal court of record in any action or proceeding based upon the same or substantially similar facts, transaction, or occurrence." But the City's moving papers make no showing that Case No. CGC-20-582151 is an "action or proceeding based upon the same or substantially similar facts, transaction, or occurrence." Because the City's moving papers do not demonstrate that any of the definitions of "vexatious litigant" under 391.1(b) apply here, the motion is denied without prejudice. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. rulings.Case Title: 2075 SUTTER STREET HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION VS. EAST BAY CONSTRUCTION & MANAGEMENT LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Compelling Attendance At Deposition And Producing Documents And Imposing Sanctions Against East Bay Construction & Management Llc
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, February 10, 2025, line 11, PLAINTIFF 2075 SUTTER STREET HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION'S Motion For Order Compelling Attendance At Deposition And Producing Documents And Imposing Sanctions Against East Bay Construction & Management Llc (Tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Plaintiff 2075 Sutter Street Homeowners Association brings this unopposed motion for an order compelling defendant East Bay Construction & Management LLC to produce its PMQ for deposition accompanied by documents described in the deposition notice. (Rojas Dec., Ex. C.) The moving papers demonstrate that Plaintiff served the deposition notice on defendant, defendant did not serve objections, and although defendant's counsel engaged in some scheduling discussion did not agree to produce a deponent within a reasonable time of the noticed day and time. Although this is a closer call because Plaintiff's statement of facts concerned the document requests is somewhat cursory (see CCP 2025.450(a)(1)), the court is satisfied based on the entire declaration the accompanying request for production is supported by good cause. Defendant is ordered to produce its PMQ deponent for deposition within 10 court days of notice of entry of this order. Plaintiff also seeks monetary and evidentiary sanctions. The court finds that defendant did not act with substantial justification and has presented no circumstances making imposition of a sanction unjust. Accordingly, sanctions are mandatory. (CCP 2025.450(g)(1).) The court grants sanctions payable by defendant to plaintiff within 10 court days of notice of entry of this order in the amount of $2,500. The court does not order evidentiary sanctions at this time as it finds that compliance with its order will avoid prejudice to Plaintiff at trial. (See Manlin v. Milner (2022) 82 Cal.App.5th 1004, 1024 ["A trial court has broad discretion to impose discovery sanctions, but they should be appropriate to the dereliction, and should not exceed that which is required to protect the interests of the party entitled to but denied discovery. A discovery sanction should only be such as is suitable and necessary to enable the party seeking discovery to obtain the objects of the discovery he seeks, and may not be designed to impose punishment."] [internal quotation marks and citation omitted].) (end of tentative ruling part 1 see part 2) = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: JASON COOK VS. CF COLLECTIVE, LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff?S Motion For Immediate Stay Of Defendants? 16 Sets Of Written Discovery And For A Protective Order; Motion For Immediate Stay And Appointment Of A Discovery Referee In The Alternative (Motions)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, February 10, 2025, line 13, 2 - PLAINTIFF JASON COOK 'S Motion For Immediate Stay Of Defendants' 16 Sets Of Written Discovery And For A Protective Order; Motion For Immediate Stay And Appointment Of A Discovery Referee In The Alternative (Motions) (tentative ruling part 1, see part 2) Plaintiff Jason Cook seeks a stay of defendants' written discovery and a protective order; in the alternative, he seeks appointment of a discovery referee. Defendants oppose all of the requested relief. The court grants the motion in part. The court finds that appointment of a referee to handle all discovery disputes among the parties is warranted in this case. The referee will be authorized to set the date, time, and place for all hearings; direct the issuance of subpoenas; preside over hearings; take evidence; and rule on objections, motions, and other requests during the course of the hearing. The court finds that exceptional circumstances exist in this case justifying appointment of the referee. Specifically, there is a great deal of discovery at issue in the present motions; the court has adjudicated discovery motions already; lengthy meet and confer efforts do not seem to advance discovery in the self-executing manner that the Discovery Act intends; and plaintiff's motion for stay of discovery and protective order points to hundreds of individual requests at issue in this motion. In short, the court finds that the discovery issues presented by the parties pose require an "inordinately time-consuming" endeavor by the court. (See Taggares v. Superior Court (1998) 62 Cal.App.4th 94, 105.) Plaintiff requests that the court appoint attorney Philip S. Ward, who has previously acted as a judge pro tem in connection with discovery in this case. Plaintiff also requests that the defendants be ordered to pay the entirety of the cost. The court instead orders the parties to meet and confer about the identity of a mutually acceptable referee, hourly rate, and extent to which court facilities or staff need to be used. (CRC 3.922(b), (f), and (g).) The parties are also ordered to meet and confer about "a fair and reasonable apportionment of reference costs." (CRC 3.922(f)(3).) The apportionment can be based on the parties' ability to pay, but the court has identified no authority permitting it to require defendants to pay referee costs as a sanction, as Plaintiff appears to contend. (end of tentative ruling part 1 see part 2) = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: MARIA CRISTINA BAEZA VARELA VS. RALPH OSTERHOUT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Change Of Venue
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, February 10, 2025, line 6, DEFENDANT RALPH OSTERHOUT'S Motion To Change Of Venue (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Defendant Ralph Osterhout's motion to transfer venue is denied. First, Osterhout has not shown venue is improper in San Francisco, such that the court must transfer the action to Sonoma County per CCP 396(b). Venue here is proper because plaintiff Maria Varela allegedly entered her employment contract in San Francisco and experienced labor violations here as well. (Varela decl. para. 7; Govt. Code 12965(c)(3) ["A [FEHA] action may be brought in any county in the state in which the unlawful practice is alleged to have been committed"].) Second, Osterhout has not shown a discretionary transfer is warranted. If an action is filed in the proper county, the court has discretion to transfer venue "[w]hen the convenience of witnesses and the ends of justice would be promoted by the change." (CCP 397(c).) Osterhout's showing is a general one: he submits a declaration that he is infirm, but no other evidence. His briefing makes general assertions about other witnesses and their convenience but he offers no evidentiary showing as to other witnesses. "Generally, the 'convenience of the parties is not to be considered upon a motion for a change of venue.' ' (Rycz v. Superior Court of San Francisco County (2022) 81 Cal.App.5th 824, 836 [Rycz] [quoting Peiser v. Mettler (1958) 50 Cal.2d 594, 612].) Instead, the court considers convenience of nonparty witnesses, but not those who are employees of the litigants. (Weil & Brown, Civil Procedure Before Trial (The Rutter Group 2024) para. 3:555, 3:558.) "Before the convenience of witnesses may be considered as a ground for an order granting a change of venue it must be shown that their proposed testimony is admissible, relevant and material to some issue in the case as shown by the record before the court. The declaration or declarations supporting the motion should set forth the names of the witnesses, the nature of the testimony expected from each, and the reasons why the attendance of each would be inconvenient." (Rycz, supra, 81 Cal.App.5th at p. 836 [internation quotations and citations omitted].) Here, Osterhout fails to meet his burden of showing transfer to Sonoma County would be most convenient for witnesses. Although the Osterhout declaration suggests it would be burdensome for him to litigate this action in San Francisco, the burdens to a party are not considered. The Osterhout declaration does not identify any specific nonparty witnesses, let alone set forth their proposed testimony or explain why attendance for each in San Francisco would be inconvenient. (Rycz, supra, 81 Cal.App.5th 824, 836.) (end of tentative ruling part 1 see part 2) = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: MARIA CRISTINA BAEZA VARELA VS. RALPH OSTERHOUT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT RALPH OSTERHOUT'S MOTION TO CHANGE OF VENUE (TENTATIVE RULING PURPOSES ONLY PART 2 OF 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, February 10, 2025, line 6, DEFENDANT RALPH OSTERHOUT'S MOTION TO CHANGE OF VENUE (TENTATIVE RULING PURPOSES ONLY PART 2 OF 2) For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. (end of tentative ruling part 2 of 2) = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: ALIGN TECHNOLOGY, INC. VS. ALLAN HSU ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For To Correct The Judgment From Erroneous $16,124.50 To The Correct Judgment To $17,124.50
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, February 10, 2025, line 7, PLAINTIFF ALIGN TECHNOLOGY, INC'S Motion For To Correct The Judgment From Erroneous $16,124.50 To The Correct Judgment To $17,124.50 Moving party Align Technology brings this unopposed motion to correct the judgment entered by this court on December 30, 2024. Specifically, the judgment entered in favor of Align on December 30, 2024 contains a mathematical error; the itemized amounts awarded by the court are added incorrectly and the listed total does not match the itemized amounts. The motion is granted. The court will issue an amended judgment awarding damages in the amount of $14,569.47, prejudgment interest at $1,185.03; attorney fees at $1000; and costs at $370, for a total of $17,124.50. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. rulings.Case Title: CRYSTAL LEI VS. DEMAS WAI YAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, February 10, 2025, line 8, DEFENDANT DEMAS YAN MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS Defendant Demas Wai Yan seeks judgment on the pleadings on plaintiffs Crystal Lei and Bryant Fu's complaint, styled as a "Verified Complaint for Action on Judgment Debts." Yan contends that all of the sanctions orders that provide the basis for Lei and Fu's complaint are already judgments. He argues that the Execution of Judgment Laws (EJL, codified at CCP 680.010 et seq.), provide the exclusive procedure for a party to renew a judgment. Yan argues that certain limits on the ability to renew a judgment-such as time requirements, and the fact that some judgments can be renewed only once-would be circumvented if Lei and Fu could roll all of the sanctions orders up into a new judgment. "[N]othing in the EJL limits a judgment creditor's right to pursue an independent action on the judgment. (sec. 683.050.) An independent action " 'based on a judgment is an action based on contract. The [original] judgment becomes a debt which the judgment debtor is obligated to pay and the law implies a contract on his part to pay it. [Citation.]' [Citation.] Thus, when successful, an action on a judgment results in the entry of a new judgment.' " (Cadle Co. II, Inc. v. Fiscus (2008) 163 Cal.App.4th 1232, 1238 [citing Goldman v. Simpson (2008) 160 Cal.App.4th 255, 262, fn. 3].) Indeed, even when a judgment cannot be renewed under the EJL, it may be enforceable in an action on the judgment. (See Green v. Zissis (1992) 5 Cal.App.4th 1219, 1222.) On its face, Plaintiffs' complaint is properly pleaded. The motion for judgment on the pleadings is denied. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CVA)
